course_description:
This course examines data analysis, design, and business issues in relation to database management, conceptual and data modelling, and business applications. Students will gain an overall understanding of the relational database model and learn to design and retrieve information from relational databases using Structured Query Language (SQL).

Data plays an integral role in decision-making within modern organisations. Therefore, data needs to be managed efficiently and effectively to enable easy, timely, informative, and accurate access to relevant and useful data. This forms the underlying framework and rationale for the learning content in BISM7206.ﾠ

This course provides students with an overall understanding of the relational database model and seeks to develop each student's ability to design and retrieve information from relational databases.

These skills will be further enhanced through learning Structured Query Language (SQL). The course seeks to develop students' knowledge and understanding of database elements, including data integrity, domains,ﾠrecovery, concurrency, security, and other similar concepts. Overall, students will learn how to: construct data models and normalised tables designed to help achieve business objectives; evaluate and apply integrity constraints including domains, primary keys, and foreign keys; understand the concept of nulls and how this concept applies to SQL queries and database design; use SQL to retrieve relevant information for business purposes; andﾠunderstand the importance of database design elements in business applications. Students will also gain an appreciation of the benefits and requirements of distributed databases, and understand the relative advantages and disadvantages of relational and object-oriented databases and how these two models can coexist.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

1- AI-supported in-class exercises have been introduced, allowing students to complete parallel manual and GenAI-assisted SQL tasks to practise core coding skills, compare outputs, and experiment with prompt-engineering techniques.

2- Additional support for the team project is provided through an iteration submission for formative tutor feedback, as well as dedicated tutorial checkpoints where teams discuss progress and seek guidance.

course_aims: This course aims to provide students with an overall understanding of the relational database model. Important database concepts, methodologies, tools and techniques are introduced to provide students with the knowledge and skills to analyse, design and develop well-structured and effective databases. Students are introduced to data modelling techniques, entity relationship diagrams, and SQL to develop their ability to design and retrieve information from relational databases. The course also aims to enhance students' understanding of data integriety, domains, recovery, concurrency, security and other similar issues considered in the practical application of database management systems.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate foundational understanding and/or critical application of data modelling techniques to design robust data structures that address complex business requirements.
Apply SQL to extract data from relational information systems, and apply generative AI tools responsibly to support database design, coding and validation.
Explain and apply principles of data quality assurance and apply techniques such as data cleaning, validation, and integrity enforcement to improve the reliability of organisational data.
Apply current Information Systems issues and effectively communicate strategic recommendations for leveraging business data for competitive advantage.
Collaborate effectively in teams and articulate complex information systems concepts through advanced written and oral communication skills.

assessment_details.title: AI debate
assessment_details.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Role play/ Simulation
assessment_details.task_description: The objective of this assessment is to evaluate students’ ability to (1) demonstrate understanding of Information Systems issues, (2) critically reflect on and articulate the implications of AI for business processes and data use, and (3) engage constructively in informed discourse on the topics. While tutorial discussions will occur in teams, each student will be assessed individually. Detailed debate motions and preparation guidelines will be posted on Blackboard. Preparation: You may use AI tools while researching and preparing written notes for the in-class debate. Use of AI tools is prohibited once the live debate begins. Please note: The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy. You need to bring your laptop to the session. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

More information will be released on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension

The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
Late submissions are not permitted. If you require additional time, you must lodge a formal extension request via the UQ website before the scheduled assessment date.

assessment_details.title: Database design
assessment_details.due_date: Iteration Session Submission - not graded. 24/04/2026 4:00 pm Final Submission 22/05/2026 4:00 pm
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed, Longitudinal. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: In this assessment, students will work in project teams of four (4) to five (5) members, with AI explicitly treated as an additional collaborative teammate, to design, develop, and implement a MySQL database system for a client scenario. The project will culminate in the submission of a professional business report that includes professional modelling diagrams, supporting documentation and SQL scripts using MySQL Workbench. This assessment task provides students with the opportunity to demonstrate: Effective collaboration and teamwork skills; A solid understanding of core database concepts , and The ability to design, develop and implement a well-structured relational database that meets defined business requirements. Tools: MySQL Workbench is required for this assessment. It can be downloaded from the UQ website, accessed via UQ’s Digital Workspace, or used directly in the BEL computer labs. Iteration Session (Week 8): Students will submit selected sections of their project by the end of Week 8. During the week 9 tutorial, students receive oral comments on their work from their tutor. This iteration submission is for feedback only and is not graded. Teamwork: Assessment Task 2 will be undertaken in teams of 4-5 students. Team members must be enrolled in the same tutorial class to allow some teamwork to be conducted during tutorials (this is a mandatory requirement and will not be open to negotiation). Students are reminded that a key Learning Objective of this course is the ability to work in teams, and this assessment task directly addresses that Learning Objective. Zero marks will apply if this requirement is not addressed. Peer assessment: Students must submit confidential self and peer evaluations to enable assessment of team member contributions. The evaluation process assists the teaching team in understanding how each student contributed, and the findings are confidential. The peer evaluation can affect each student's mark for this assignment. Further Information: Assessment guidelines, along with team requirements, the case study description, marking rubric, and specifications will be available under the Assessment link on Blackboard. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submissions must be uploaded through the provided Blackboard links. Please follow the detailed instructions provided on Blackboard for further information.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Course Overview and Intro to DBMS Introduction to database management, including traditional file-based systems, the database management system and fundamental database concepts. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 1, 2) Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Entity Relationship Modelling Entity relationship modelling is explored in terms of a data driven approach using entity-relationship diagrams including design elements such as entities, relationships, attributes, keys and multiplicity. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 12 and 13) Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Tutorial 1: Data & Information; MYSQL prep Students will explore the difference between data and information, examine the 3-level database architecture through discussion and case analysis, and begin preparing for practical database work by setting up and navigating the MySQL environment. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Relational Model Relational model concepts are discussed including specific terminology, keys, views and integrity constraints. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 4) Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Tutorial 2: Entity Relationship Modelling Students will identify entities and relationships in real-world scenarios, review key concepts such as multiplicity and participation constraints, and apply their understanding through practical ER modelling exercises. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Structured Query Language (I) Introduction to basic SQL DDL and DML statements. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7) Learning outcomes: L02

Tutorial 3: Relational Modelling Students will work through practical exercises on relations, keys, and integrity constraints, and apply these concepts by creating a relational model and corresponding relational schema. Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Structured Query Language (II) Exploring aggregation and grouping, and relational operations to enable search conditions and order/grouping of results. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7) Learning outcomes: L02

Tutorial 4: SQL Pactical Exercises (1) This tutorial focuses on foundational SQL skills through practical exercises in MySQL, starting with basic queries and manual command-line operations. Students will then engage in AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate, interpret, and refine SQL queries. Learning outcomes: L02
AI Debate Week – No Lecture Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Tutorial 5: In-class AI debate (Assignment 1) This tutorial is dedicated to Assignment 1, where students will participate in an in-class AI debate, critically examining the role of AI in data management and information systems. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
In-Semester Break

Normalisation Explore how normalisation supports database design and how concepts such as partial, transitive, and functional dependencies enable the logical database design to transition and conform to particular normal forms. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 14,15) Learning outcomes: L02

Tutorial 6: SQL practices (II) This tutorial focuses on advancing SQL skills using MySQL Workbench, with an emphasis on tackling complex queries and clarifying challenging concepts. Students will also explore AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate and refine advanced SQL queries, enhancing their problem-solving and query optimization abilities. Learning outcomes: L03

Data Quality and Cleaning Data is a valuable resource and access to accurate, current and informative data is critical to business success. Therefore, data needs to be managed and organised like other business assets. Learning outcomes: L03

Tutorial 7: Normalisation exercises Students will engage in practical exercises to identify functional dependencies and apply normalization techniques up to 3NF, reinforcing their understanding of data structure optimization and redundancy reduction. Learning outcomes: L02
Guest Lecture Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

Tutorial 8: Assignment Iteration feedback and Data cleaning This tutorial focuses on providing feedback on Assignment 1 iterations and developing data cleaning skills. Students will identify and resolve common data quality issues to prepare datasets for accurate analysis and reporting. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05

Transaction Management This lecture explores the critical role of transaction management in business systems, focusing on how organisations maintain data integrity and consistency in multi-user environments and recover from failures to ensure reliable operations. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 22) Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Tutorial 9: Team project This tutorial is dedicated to the team project, providing students with structured time to work in their groups, develop project deliverables. Labour Day Public Holiday - Monday 4th May 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05

Database Security and Control We’ll look at why database security matters, the main threats to data security, and the core ideas of confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the CIA triad). We’ll also cover practical countermeasures and finish with an overview of Data Control Language (DCL). Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 20) Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Tutorial 10: Database Security and team project This tutorial covers practical exercises in database security, focusing on access control and user permissions. Students will also continue working on their team projects. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Distributed Database Management Systems We examine distributed DBMSs including concepts, problems, technologies and protocols. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 24, 25) Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Tutorial 11: Team Project This tutorial is dedicated to the team project, providing students with structured time to work in their groups, develop project deliverables, and receive feedback on their progress. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
Overview and Big Data & Technologies Exploring why data management technologies and approaches have expanded beyond relational databases and data warehousing. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Tutorial 12: Exam consult Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
